title: Firms’ Short-Run Decisions to Produce and Long-Run Decisions to Enter or Exit a Market
description: In this exercise, practice what you've learned about the difference between entry and exit and the shut-down rule.
topic_path: Firm entry, exit, and the shut-down rule
Firms’ Short-Run Decisions to Produce and Long-Run Decisions to Enter or Exit a Market

assessment_items.question_content:
Pawel is a consultant for an economic advisory service. He currently produces $10$ hours per week of consultancy services. The price of an hour of his services is $\$60$, and the market for consultancy services is perfectly competitive. The table below shows his costs associated with producing $10$ hours of services:

Type of cost | Cost
-|-
$MC(10)$ | $\$60$
$ATC(10)$ | $\$80$
$AVC(10)$ | $\$50$

**If Pawel wants to maximize profit, what is his best course of action in the short run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An economic agent increases output if they can earn more on an additional unit than it costs them and decreases output if it costs them more to produce an additional unit that it earns in revenue. Therefore, the best choice is always the quantity where the cost of producing another unit equals the additional revenue from another unit.
The cost of producing another unit is marginal cost and the revenue earned from an additional unit is marginal revenue.  
When price is constant, marginal revenue is constant. Therefore, Pawel's marginal revenue is $\$60$.

Pawel should produce $10$ hours of services.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the shutdown rule?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The shutdown rule is a short run decision tool a firm can use to decide whether it produces anything in the short run.
A firm should continue producing if it can cover its fixed costs, and this is true when $P>AVC$. Shutting down when a firm can cover its fixed costs increases a firm’s losses.

A firm can cover its variable cost if its price is greater than average variable cost (AVC).
Choose $Q=0$ when $P<AVC$

choices.content: Exit the industry when $P<\text{Average variable cost}$
Exit the industry when $P<\text{Average total cost}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The market for boxed pasta is perfectly competitive. Currently, the price of a box of pasta is less than average total cost (ATC) but greater than average variable cost (AVC). 

**Which of the following occurs in the short-run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If a firm’s price is less than average total cost, it is making economic losses.
A firm exits an industry in the long run if it is making economic losses.
However, a firm is willing to produce in the short run as long as it is covering its variable cost
A firm covers its variable cost if $P>AVC$. These firms can get a price higher than AVC.

The answer:

> Produce in the short run.

choices.content: Firms produce in the short run.
Firms produce the output where average total cost is lowest.

assessment_items.question_content:
The cost and revenue curves for a perfectly competitive industry are shown in this graph:

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2f2d70e0ccf177a1995422cd43ed398a606628de)

**What is the price in this market and what quantity will this firm produce in the short run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The shutdown rule is a short run decision tool a firm can use to decide whether it produces anything in the short run.

If a firm can cover its variable costs, it should produce. Shutting down will increase a firm’s losses.

A firm can cover its variable cost if its price is greater than average variable cost ($AVC$).
If $P>AVC$ at any quantity, then a firm should choose the quantity where the marginal cost curve and the marginal revenue curve intersect.
Price is $P_3$ in this market because there is a horizontal marginal revenue curve at $P_3$. 

$P_3$ is greater than average variable cost at several quantities, so this firm should not shut down.
The MR curve intersects the $MC$ curve at $Q_3$. 
The answer:

$P_3; Q_2$

choices.content: $P_3$; this firm will shut down in the short run
$P_2$; this firm will exit the industry

assessment_items.question_content:
The cost curves for a firm in a perfectly competitive market are shown in this graph:

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/65ad30b4b5d2554c47410bb2e782af35f5fc3351)

**All of the following quantity and price combinations are on this firm’s short-run supply curve EXCEPT**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A firm’s short-run supply curve contains all of the quantities that a firm is willing to sell at various prices.

A firm is willing to sell at a loss as long as the price is higher than its lowest variable cost.

A firm is not willing to sell at a price lower than its lowest variable cost.
A firm always chooses a quantity on its marginal cost curve because the optimal quantity is the quantity where marginal cost equals marginal revenue.

A firm’s short-run supply curve is its marginal cost curve above the minimum $AVC$ and quantity equals zero for any price lower than the minimum of $AVC$.
The answer:

$\$5$; $20$

This price is lower than the minimum of $AVC$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Anka runs a water sports service in a perfectly competitive beach town. The price she is able to charge for her services varies from day to day, but she has no control over that price. Her cost and revenue curves for a perfectly competitive industry are shown in this graph:

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a17e1d70a54e17c0d3e81eee1c641b932d571ede)

**What is the lowest price at which Anka would be willing to produce in the short run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A firm will not shut down in the short run if it can at least cover its variable costs.

A firm covers its variable cost if it can get a price higher than its average variable cost.

Any price higher than the minimum $AVC$ will induce this firm to produce.
The minimum average $AVC$ is at $\$55$.
The answer:

$\$55$

assessment_items.question_content:
The cost and revenue curves for a perfectly competitive industry are shown in this graph:

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ae98b8a11f83cea41c686b6964f38b98ce7e3bec)

**What is the quantity that this firm will choose in the short run, and what is its economic profit or loss?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The firm chooses the quantity where the marginal revenue ($MR$) and marginal cost ($MC$) curves intersect as long as that intersection is higher than the $AVC$ curve. Use this formula to calculate economic profit:

$\text{Profit} = (\text{Price}-ATC) \times \text{Quantity}$


$MC$ and $MR$ intersect at a quantity of $9$.
At a quantity of $9$, the price is higher than the average variable cost of $9$ units, so the firm chooses to produce $9$ units rather than shut down.
$\text{Price}=\$90$, $ATC(9)=\$140$, and $\text{Quantity}=9$:

$\begin{align} \text{Profit}& = Q \times (P-ATC)\\\\
& = 9 \times (\$90-\$140)\\\\
& = 9 \times (-\$50) \\\\
& = -\$450\end{align}$

The firm chooses to produce 9 units and earns economic losses of $\$450$:

choices.content: This firm will shut down in the short run and make an economic profit of $0$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The cost and revenue curves for a perfectly competitive industry are shown in this graph:


![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c1446a7e4454667094453a027284b1e7f80a5798)

**What best describes how a firm would respond in the short-run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This firm has a horizontal marginal revenue curve which indicates the firm has no control over price. They will get the same price for any quantity they choose to produce.

Price is below average variable cost for any possible quantity.
The shutdown rule states a firm should choose a quantity of zero, in other words shut down, if price is less than average variable cost.

The answer:
>Shut down

choices.content: Increase output to decrease average total cost

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes what is true for a firm to be willing to produce in both the short run and the long run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A firm is willing to sell in the short run if it can cover its variable costs.
A firm covers its variable cost if price is greater than average variable cost.
A firm is willing to sell in the long run if it earns either profits of zero (also called **normal economic profit**) or positive profits.
A firm earns normal economic profit when price equals average total cost and positive economic profit when price is greater than average total cost.

The answer:

$P>=AVC$ in the short run; $P>=ATC$ in the long run.

choices.content: $P\geq AVC$ in the short run; $P\geq ATC$ in the long run.
$P\geq AVC$ in the short run; $P\leq ATC$ in the long run.
$P=AVC$ in the short run; $P<ATC$ in the long run
$P<AVC$ in the short run; $P\geq ATC$ in the long run
$P<AVC$ in the short run; $P\leq ATC$ in the long run

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following would entice a firm to enter an industry?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Firms exit an industry in the long run when price is less than average total cost.
Firms enter an industry when there are profits to be made.
The answer:

$P>ATC$

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is true about the price of a good that leads firms to exit an industry?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Firms enter an industry when there are profits to be made. Firms make positive economic profits when $P>ATC$.
If a firm earns zero economic profits (also called **normal economic profits**), it has no incentive to enter or exit the industry.

Firms exit an industry when they make economic losses in the long run.
Firms exit an industry in the long run when price is less than average total cost because when P<ATC a firm is earning economic losses.

The answer:

$P<ATC$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes what happens in the long run when a firm anticipates that the price of their good will always be less than average total cost (ATC)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Firms enter an industry when there are profits to be made. Firms make positive economic profits when P>ATC.

If a firm earns zero economic profits (also called **normal economic profits**), it has no incentive to enter or exit the industry.

Firms exit an industry when they make economic losses in the long run.

Firms exit an industry in the long run when price is less than average total cost because when P<ATC a firm is earning economic losses.


The answer:
>The firm will exit the industry.

choices.content: The firm will exit the industry.
The firm will produce as long as price is greater than average variable cost.
The firm will enter the industry
The firm will produce as long as price is less than average variable cost.

assessment_items.question_content:
The cost and revenue curves for a perfectly competitive industry are shown in this graph:


![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a0945e3ae84c7612fc0fddffb27fffdc2ab39d7b)

**What happens to the number of firms and the market supply curve in this market if the price of this good stays at $\$90$ in the long run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
If price is less than average total cost, firms are making economic losses.
If firms earn losses in the long run, they exit the industry.
This firm will exit the industry in the long run, as will other firms also experiencing losses.
The number of firms in a market or industry is a determinant of market supply.
When firms exit, there are fewer firms and the market supply decreases.
The answer:

>The number of firms decreases; market supply decreases

choices.content: The number of firms decreases; market supply decreases.
One firm remains in the market; market supply is unchanged.
The number of firms decreases; the market supply is unaffected.
The number of firms stays the same; the market supply decreases
The number of firms increases; the market supply increases.

assessment_items.question_content:
The cost and marginal revenue curves for a firm in a perfectly competitive market are shown in this graph. 

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/65ad30b4b5d2554c47410bb2e782af35f5fc3351)

**What price will convince other firms to enter this industry?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

The cost and marginal revenue curves for a firm in a perfectly competitive market are shown in this graph. 

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/65ad30b4b5d2554c47410bb2e782af35f5fc3351)

**At which price do other firms have no incentive to enter or exit this market?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A firm has no incentive to enter a market if it will not earn a profit.
A firm has no incentive to exit a market if it isn’t losing money.
A firm has no incentive to exit or enter a market if it will earn zero economic profit. “Normal economic profit” is another way of saying zero economic profit.
A firm earns normal economic profit if price equals average total cost. A perfectly competitive firm earns zero economic profit when price is equal to the minimum of average total cost.

This firm’s minimum average total cost is \$130.
The answer:

 $\$130$

assessment_items.question_content:
The cost and marginal revenue curves for a firm in a perfectly competitive market are shown in this graph. 

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/65ad30b4b5d2554c47410bb2e782af35f5fc3351)


**What price will lead to an increase in the market supply of this good in the long run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The market supply increases if firms enter the market.
Firms enter the market if they will make an economic profit.

Firms make economic profits if price is greater than average total cost.

At a price of \$214, price is greater than average total cost. The firm would choose to make 14 units and would earn economic profits.
The answer:

> $\$214$
